name: First Aid for Fever
when_to_apply: Temperature above 38°C (100.4°F); body feeling hot; chills and sweating
immediate_steps: Make the person rest in a cool, well-ventilated room
Remove excess clothing and heavy blankets
Place a cool (not ice cold) wet cloth on forehead, neck, and armpits
Give paracetamol (500 mg adult / 10–15 mg/kg child) if available
Encourage drinking plenty of water, coconut water, or ORS
Sponge the body with lukewarm water (not cold — causes shivering and raises temperature)
Monitor temperature every 30 minutes
home_remedies: Tulsi (Holy Basil) tea — boil 10–15 tulsi leaves in water; add honey and ginger; sip warm
Ginger and honey water — boil grated ginger in water, strain, add honey; effective anti-inflammatory
Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) juice or kadha — traditional immunity booster used in Indian homes
Haldi doodh (Turmeric milk) — 1/2 teaspoon turmeric in warm milk; anti-inflammatory and soothing
Methi (Fenugreek) seed water — soak 1 tsp methi seeds overnight; drink in morning; known fever reducer
Cold wet sock therapy — wet socks wrung out and worn to bed to stimulate circulation and cool body
do_not_do: Do not give aspirin to children — risk of Reye's syndrome
Do not use ice or ice-cold water — causes rapid shivering and worsens internal temperature
Do not over-bundle the person in blankets
Do not force feed if the person is nauseous
go_to_doctor_if: Fever above 39.5°C (103°F) in adults or 38.5°C (101.3°F) in infants under 3 months
Fever lasting more than 3 days
Fever with rash, stiff neck, confusion, or difficulty breathing
Fever in child with febrile seizure
Fever with no obvious cause in immunocompromised patient

name: First Aid for Common Cold
when_to_apply: Runny nose, sneezing, nasal congestion, mild sore throat, no or low-grade fever
immediate_steps: Rest at home — avoid going out in cold weather
Stay well hydrated — warm water, soups, herbal teas
Use saline nasal drops or spray to relieve blocked nose
Gargle with warm salt water (1/2 tsp salt in 1 glass warm water) for sore throat
Steam inhalation with or without menthol/eucalyptus drops for nasal congestion
Take OTC antihistamine (cetirizine) for runny nose if needed
home_remedies: Kadha (Indian herbal decoction) — boil tulsi leaves, ginger, black pepper, cloves, cinnamon in water; strain and add honey; highly effective for cold and cough
Haldi doodh (Turmeric milk / Golden Milk) — warm milk with 1/2 tsp turmeric; fights infection and soothes throat
Adrak chai (Ginger tea) — fresh ginger boiled with tea; natural decongestant and antibiotic
Honey and black pepper — 1 tsp honey + pinch of black pepper; reduces mucus and soothes throat
Ajwain (Carom seed) steam — boil ajwain seeds in water; inhale steam; clears nasal passage naturally
Mustard oil nasal drops — 2 drops warm mustard oil in each nostril; traditional Indian remedy for blocked nose
Garlic and honey — crush 1–2 garlic cloves with honey; antimicrobial; given to children by Indian mothers
do_not_do: Do not take antibiotics — cold is viral; antibiotics do not help
Do not use nasal decongestant spray for more than 3–5 days
Do not give cold medicine combinations to children under 6 without doctor advice
Do not go to work or school — spreads infection
go_to_doctor_if: Cold lasting more than 10 days without improvement
High fever above 39°C with cold symptoms
Green or yellow nasal discharge with facial pain (may be sinusitis)
Earache or hearing loss developing
Chest pain or shortness of breath

name: First Aid for Cough
when_to_apply: Dry or wet cough from cold, throat irritation, dust, or mild infection
immediate_steps: Identify if dry cough (tickling, no mucus) or wet cough (mucus, productive)
Drink warm water or herbal tea to soothe throat irritation
For wet cough — steam inhalation to loosen mucus
For dry cough — honey and warm water reduces irritation
Avoid cold drinks, ice cream, and air conditioning during cough
Use OTC cough syrup (dextromethorphan for dry; ambroxol/guaifenesin for wet)
home_remedies: Honey and ginger juice — mix equal parts; 1 tsp 3 times daily; one of the most effective natural cough suppressants
Mulethi (Licorice root) and honey — crush mulethi root, boil in water, add honey; soothes bronchial lining
Tulsi, ginger and black pepper decoction — Indian kitchen staple; boil together, strain, sip hot with honey
Paan (betel leaf) with ajwain and mishri — traditional Indian remedy; fold ajwain and mishri in betel leaf, chew slowly; relieves cough
Vicks VapoRub chest rub — applied on chest, back, and under nose; menthol relieves cough at night
Clove and honey — 2 cloves boiled in water or chewed with honey; antibacterial and soothing
Haldi and warm milk — classic Indian mom remedy; turmeric's curcumin has anti-inflammatory properties for bronchial cough
do_not_do: Do not give honey to infants under 12 months — botulism risk
Do not give codeine-based cough syrups to children under 12
Do not suppress a productive (wet) cough completely — it clears infection
Do not continue home remedy beyond 5 days if cough persists
go_to_doctor_if: Cough lasting more than 3 weeks
Coughing up blood
Cough with high fever and difficulty breathing
Whooping sound after coughing fits (especially in children)
Cough in a smoker with weight loss — rule out lung cancer

name: First Aid for Heart Attack
when_to_apply: Crushing chest pain, pain radiating to left arm or jaw, sweating, nausea, breathlessness, feeling of impending doom
immediate_steps: CALL 108 / 112 (emergency ambulance) IMMEDIATELY — do not drive yourself
Make the person sit or lie down in a comfortable position (semi-reclined with head and shoulders raised)
Loosen tight clothing — collar, tie, belt
Give 300 mg Aspirin tablet to chew (not swallow whole) if not allergic and no bleeding history
If person is unconscious and not breathing — start CPR: 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths
If an AED (defibrillator) is available — use it immediately with instructions
Stay with the person and keep them calm; reassure them help is coming
Do not give food, water, or any other medication
home_remedies: No home remedy is appropriate for heart attack — this is a medical emergency
Chewing aspirin 300 mg is the only evidence-based pre-hospital intervention
Keep the person calm and still — activity increases oxygen demand on damaged heart
Garlic and arjuna bark (terminalia arjuna) are traditional heart tonics — NOT for acute attack but used in Indian tradition for heart health maintenance
do_not_do: Do not let the person walk or exert themselves
Do not give water or food — patient may need emergency surgery
Do not delay calling ambulance by trying home remedies
Do not leave the person alone
Do not give nitroglycerin unless prescribed specifically for that person
go_to_doctor_if: Any chest pain lasting more than 5 minutes — go to emergency immediately
Chest pain with sweating, nausea, or arm/jaw pain — emergency
Palpitations with dizziness or fainting
Even if pain resolves — get ECG done same day

name: First Aid for Fracture (Broken Bone)
when_to_apply: After fall or accident with severe pain, swelling, deformity, inability to move limb, bone visible through skin
immediate_steps: DO NOT try to straighten or move the broken bone
Immobilize the injured limb in the position found — use rolled newspaper, magazine, or wooden plank as a splint
Tie the splint with cloth above and below the fracture — not over the fracture site
Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling (do not apply ice directly to skin)
Elevate the injured limb above heart level if possible to reduce swelling
For open fracture (bone visible) — cover wound with clean cloth; do not push bone back
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain if available and person is conscious
Transport to hospital immediately — call 108 for spine, neck, or pelvis fractures (do not move)
home_remedies: Haldi (Turmeric) paste — apply on swollen area around fracture for pain and swelling; anti-inflammatory (not on open wounds)
Castor oil wrap — warm castor oil cloth wrapped around injured area to reduce swelling; traditional Indian home remedy
Arandi (Castor) leaf warm compress — heated leaves applied around swollen joint; used in rural India for sprains and mild fractures
Chuna (Calcium lime) in paan mixed with milk — traditional Indian bone healing remedy; provides calcium
do_not_do: Do not try to set or push back the broken bone
Do not apply ice directly on skin — use cloth wrap
Do not move a person with suspected spine or neck fracture
Do not give food or water if surgery may be required
Do not remove embedded objects from open fracture wounds
go_to_doctor_if: Any suspected fracture requires X-ray and medical evaluation
Numbness or blueness below the fracture site — nerve or blood vessel injury
Open fracture (bone visible) — emergency
Suspected spine, pelvis, or skull fracture — call ambulance, do not move
Child with arm or leg pain after fall — even if they can move it

name: First Aid for Burns
when_to_apply: After contact with flame, hot liquid, steam, hot surface, chemical, or electricity causing skin damage
immediate_steps: Remove the person from the source of burn immediately — turn off power for electrical burns
Cool the burn under cool running water for 20 minutes — this is the most important step
Remove clothing and jewelry near the burn area (unless stuck to skin)
Cover with a clean non-fluffy cloth, plastic wrap, or cling film — do not use cotton wool
Do not burst blisters — infection risk
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain relief
Elevate burned limb if possible
home_remedies: Aloe vera gel — fresh aloe vera leaf gel applied to mild burns; cools and promotes healing; widely used in Indian homes
Coconut oil — applied after 24 hours on minor burns; moisturizes and reduces scarring; traditional South Indian remedy
Haldi (Turmeric) paste — antimicrobial; applied on minor burns in Indian households to prevent infection
Burn-specific ointment (Burnol) — Indian pharmacy staple; contains turmeric extract; first aid for minor burns
do_not_do: Do not apply toothpaste, butter, ghee, oil, or ice on burns — worsens damage and infection
Do not use ice or ice-cold water — causes additional tissue damage
Do not remove clothing stuck to the burn
Do not burst blisters with needle or hands
Do not wrap too tightly — allows for swelling
go_to_doctor_if: Burn larger than palm size (1% of body area)
Burns on face, hands, feet, genitals, or over joints
Deep burns with white, brown, or black skin (third degree)
Chemical or electrical burns — always
Burns in children, elderly, or diabetics — always

name: First Aid for Choking
when_to_apply: Person clutching throat, unable to speak or cry, turning blue, high-pitched breathing or silent, unable to cough forcefully
immediate_steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' — if they can speak or cough, encourage forceful coughing
If unable to cough or speak — stand behind the person; give 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand
If back blows fail — perform Heimlich maneuver: stand behind, make fist above navel, other hand over fist, give 5 sharp inward-upward thrusts
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until object dislodged or person loses consciousness
If unconscious — lower to floor, call 108, start CPR — each time you open airway, look for the object
For infants (under 1 year) — face-down 5 back blows + face-up 5 chest thrusts (NOT Heimlich)
home_remedies: Forceful back slaps between shoulder blades — used in Indian homes when a child swallows something
Drinking water (only if partial blockage and person can swallow) — may help dislodge mild food obstruction
Turning infant upside down and patting back — traditional Indian reflex (modified Heimlich principle)
do_not_do: Do not do blind finger sweeps in the mouth — can push object deeper
Do not give water if person cannot swallow
Do not perform Heimlich on infants under 1 year — use back blows and chest thrusts
Do not delay — brain damage starts within 4 minutes of oxygen deprivation
go_to_doctor_if: Object is dislodged but person is still coughing — may be residual fragment
Person loses consciousness — emergency 108
Child suspected to have swallowed small object — X-ray required
Any breathing difficulty after choking episode

name: First Aid for Bleeding / Cut Wound
when_to_apply: Cut, laceration, stab wound, or injury with active bleeding
immediate_steps: Wash hands before touching the wound if possible
Apply firm direct pressure on wound with clean cloth, bandage, or pad — do not remove cloth even if soaked; add more on top
Elevate injured limb above heart level to slow bleeding
If bleeding is severe from a limb — apply tourniquet 2–3 inches above wound (tight enough to stop bleeding); note time of application
Clean wound gently with clean water after bleeding slows
Apply antiseptic (povidone-iodine or Dettol diluted) to clean wound
Cover with sterile bandage or clean cloth
home_remedies: Haldi (Turmeric) powder — sprinkle on minor bleeding cuts; antimicrobial and promotes clotting; classic Indian first aid
Aloe vera gel — applied after cleaning for minor cuts; antimicrobial and skin healing
Honey — applied to clean wounds; natural antimicrobial; used traditionally in Indian homes before antiseptics
Neem leaves paste — crushed fresh neem leaves applied to minor cuts; antibacterial properties
Coconut oil — applied on clean minor wounds to prevent infection and promote healing
do_not_do: Do not remove large embedded objects — stabilize and go to hospital
Do not probe or explore deep wounds at home
Do not apply tourniquet to neck, chest, or abdomen
Do not use cotton wool directly on wounds — fibers stick to the wound
Do not remove blood-soaked bandage — add more on top to maintain clotting
go_to_doctor_if: Bleeding does not stop after 10–15 minutes of firm pressure
Deep gaping wound that may need stitches
Wound from animal bite or rusty object — tetanus risk
Wound with numbness below it — possible nerve damage
Wound in face, hand, or genitals — specialist care

name: First Aid for Snake Bite
when_to_apply: After snake bite with fang marks, pain, swelling, bleeding from bite, vision changes, muscle weakness, difficulty breathing
immediate_steps: Keep the person calm and completely still — movement accelerates venom spread through lymphatics
Immobilize the bitten limb at or below heart level — use improvised splint if needed
Remove watches, rings, bracelets near bite — swelling will make removal impossible
Mark the edge of swelling with pen and time — helps doctors track venom spread
Transport to hospital IMMEDIATELY with person lying still — carry do not walk
Note appearance of snake if possible (color, markings) — helps identify venom type
If possible — take photo of snake; do NOT try to capture or kill it
home_remedies: No effective home remedy exists for venomous snake bite — antivenom is the ONLY treatment
Traditional Indian remedies (black stone, suction, herbs) have NO scientific evidence and delay life-saving antivenom
Keeping the person calm and immobile is the most important pre-hospital action
do_not_do: Do NOT cut and suck the wound — causes infection and venom spread into rescuer
Do NOT apply tourniquet — causes more tissue damage than the venom
Do NOT apply ice, electricity, potassium permanganate, or any substance to bite
Do NOT give alcohol — speeds venom absorption
Do NOT apply black stone or traditional snake stone remedies
Do NOT try to catch or kill the snake — more bites occur this way
go_to_doctor_if: All snake bites require hospital evaluation — even if non-venomous
Any swelling, bruising, or pain — go immediately
Drooping eyelids, difficulty swallowing, or breathing — rush to emergency
Abnormal bleeding from bite or gums — coagulopathy from venom

name: First Aid for Seizure / Epileptic Fit
when_to_apply: Person suddenly falling, body shaking or convulsing, eyes rolling back, loss of consciousness, jaw clenching
immediate_steps: Stay calm — most seizures stop on their own within 1–3 minutes
Clear the area of sharp or hard objects — prevent injury during convulsion
Place something soft (folded cloth, jacket) under the person's head
Turn the person on their side (recovery position) to prevent choking on saliva or vomit
Loosen tight clothing around neck and chest
Time the seizure — if it lasts more than 5 minutes, call 108 (status epilepticus)
After seizure — let person rest; they will be confused (postictal state); reassure them gently
home_remedies: Calm environment — reduce noise, light stimulation; seizure-prone individuals are sensitive
Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) — traditional Indian nerve tonic used in Ayurveda for epilepsy management (NOT during acute seizure)
Shankhpushpi — traditional Indian herb for neurological calming (maintenance use only; not acute treatment)
do_not_do: Do NOT hold the person down — you cannot stop the seizure and may cause injury
Do NOT put anything in the mouth — person cannot swallow their tongue (a myth); you will get bitten
Do NOT give water during the seizure
Do NOT leave the person alone during or immediately after seizure
Do NOT restrain the shaking limbs — let seizure run its course
go_to_doctor_if: First-ever seizure in anyone — always
Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes — emergency (status epilepticus)
Two or more seizures without recovery in between
Person does not regain consciousness after seizure
Seizure in a pregnant woman, diabetic, or after head injury

name: First Aid for Drowning
when_to_apply: Person pulled from water; unconscious or semi-conscious; not breathing; blue lips; water from mouth
immediate_steps: Remove person from water safely — do not jump in unless trained to swim and rescue
Call 108 immediately
Check for responsiveness and breathing
If not breathing — start CPR immediately: 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths
For drowning victims — start with 5 rescue breaths before chest compressions (airway clearance priority)
Continue CPR until ambulance arrives or person breathes
Keep person warm — remove wet clothing and cover with blanket (hypothermia risk)
Place in recovery position once breathing returns (unconscious but breathing)
home_remedies: No home remedy — this is a medical emergency requiring CPR and hospitalization
Warming with blankets to prevent hypothermia — a practical first step after CPR
Jaggery and warm water (Indian home) — only after full recovery of consciousness; provides energy
do_not_do: Do not hang person upside down to drain water — wastes time and delays CPR
Do not try to remove water from lungs — the priority is CPR and breathing
Do not leave person alone even after apparent recovery — secondary drowning can occur 1–24 hours later
Do not skip hospital visit after near-drowning — internal water in lungs causes delayed deterioration
go_to_doctor_if: All drowning or near-drowning cases — hospital admission mandatory
Person was unconscious even briefly — possible secondary drowning
Coughing, breathing difficulty, or confusion after water rescue

name: First Aid for Heat Stroke
when_to_apply: Body temperature above 40°C (104°F), hot dry skin (no sweating), confusion, slurred speech, unconsciousness after heat exposure
immediate_steps: Move person to cool shaded area or air-conditioned room immediately
Call 108 — heat stroke is a medical emergency
Remove excess clothing
Apply cold water or ice packs to neck, armpits, and groin (major blood vessels)
Fan the person aggressively while spraying cool water on skin
Do not give fluids by mouth if unconscious or confused
If conscious and alert — give cold water or ORS to drink slowly
Place in recovery position if unconscious
home_remedies: Aam panna (Raw mango cooler) — traditional Indian summer drink; prevents heat stroke; mix raw mango pulp with cumin, salt, sugar, and water
Kokum sherbet — South Indian and coastal Indian summer cooler; naturally cooling
Sattu (Roasted gram flour) drink — Bihar/UP traditional summer drink; cooling and nutritious
Buttermilk (Chaas) with jeera and sendha namak — cooling probiotic drink; widely used across India in summer
Onion — cut raw onion applied to back of neck and soles of feet; traditional Indian heat remedy
do_not_do: Do not give aspirin or paracetamol — does not work for heat stroke fever (it is not infection-related fever)
Do not apply ice directly to skin — causes vasoconstriction and worsens core cooling
Do not give alcohol — worsens dehydration
Do not leave person in the sun or hot vehicle
go_to_doctor_if: Any suspected heat stroke — always emergency
Temperature above 40°C (104°F)
Person is confused, seizuring, or unconscious
Temperature does not come down despite cooling efforts

name: First Aid for Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis)
when_to_apply: After food, drug, or insect sting; hives, throat swelling, difficulty breathing, low blood pressure, loss of consciousness
immediate_steps: Call 108 immediately — anaphylaxis can kill within minutes
If EpiPen (epinephrine auto-injector) is available — inject into outer thigh immediately
Lay person flat with legs elevated (shock position) — unless breathing is difficult, then sit up
If unconscious and not breathing — start CPR
If prescribed, give antihistamine (cetirizine/chlorpheniramine) and oral steroid — NOT substitutes for epinephrine, only adjuncts
Identify and remove the allergen if possible (remove bee stinger with credit card scrape — not pinch)
home_remedies: NO home remedy for anaphylaxis — this is a medical emergency
For mild allergic reactions (hives, sneezing, itching) only: cetirizine 10 mg is appropriate
Cold compress on hives — temporary itch relief while going to doctor
do_not_do: Do not give antihistamine alone for anaphylaxis — it is too slow and too weak
Do not leave the person sitting upright if they have low blood pressure
Do not give anything by mouth if throat is swelling
Do not delay — epinephrine must be given within minutes for effect
go_to_doctor_if: All anaphylaxis cases — even after epinephrine injection (biphasic reaction possible 4–8 hours later)
Any throat tightness, difficulty breathing, or swallowing after allergen exposure
New widespread hives with swelling

name: First Aid for Eye Injury / Chemical Splash
when_to_apply: Foreign body in eye, chemical splash, smoke exposure, eye trauma, sudden pain or vision loss
immediate_steps: For chemical splash — flush eye with large amounts of clean water for 15–20 minutes continuously; hold eyelid open
For foreign body — do not rub; blink several times; flush with clean water; pull upper lid over lower to dislodge
For blunt trauma — apply ice pack wrapped in cloth (do not press on eye directly)
Cover injured eye with clean cloth or eye pad — do not use cotton wool
Seek emergency care for chemical burns, embedded object, or penetrating injury
Remove contact lenses immediately if worn
home_remedies: Rose water (Gulab jal) — a drop or two in the eye relieves mild irritation; used in Indian homes for red or irritated eyes
Triphala water eye wash — traditional Ayurvedic eyewash for mild eye fatigue and irritation (boil triphala, strain thoroughly, cool, use as wash)
Cold milk compress — cotton soaked in cold milk placed on closed eyes; reduces puffiness and mild irritation
do_not_do: Do not rub the eye with hands — may scratch cornea or push foreign body deeper
Do not remove embedded object from eye — cover and go to hospital
Do not apply eye drops (except plain saline/water rinse) without diagnosis
Do not patch both eyes — person needs one eye to navigate
go_to_doctor_if: Chemical splash — always go to eye emergency after flushing
Foreign body not dislodged after flushing
Sudden vision loss or blurring
Penetrating eye injury — emergency
Eye pain not resolving after flushing

name: First Aid for Loose Motion (Diarrhea)
when_to_apply: Frequent watery stools, stomach cramps, nausea, weakness from fluid loss
immediate_steps: Start ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) immediately — 1 sachet in 200 mL water; sip frequently
Continue eating light foods — khichdi, curd rice, banana, toast (BRAT diet)
Avoid milk, heavy oily foods, raw vegetables during diarrhea
Give zinc 20 mg/day in children under 5 years alongside ORS
Monitor urine output — reduced urine = dehydration warning
home_remedies: Rice kanji (rice water) — water from boiled rice with salt; traditional Indian first remedy for loose motion; provides electrolytes and is easy on gut
Dahi (Curd/Yogurt) with rice — probiotics from curd help restore gut flora; staple Indian remedy for diarrhea
Jeera (Cumin) water — boil 1 tsp cumin seeds in water, strain and drink; reduces gut spasms and diarrhea
Saunf (Fennel) tea — boil fennel seeds, strain, drink warm; soothes intestinal cramping
Pomegranate juice — 1 glass fresh pomegranate juice; natural astringent; used by Indian mothers for loose motion
Banana — easy to digest; provides potassium lost through diarrhea; binding effect on stools
Coconut water — natural electrolyte drink; replaces lost minerals without adding sugar load
do_not_do: Do not give loperamide (Imodium) to children under 2 or if blood in stool
Do not stop fluids — most important treatment is hydration
Do not give antibiotics without knowing the cause
Do not give sports drinks (too much sugar, wrong electrolyte balance)
go_to_doctor_if: Blood or mucus in stool
Fever above 38.5°C with diarrhea
Diarrhea more than 6 times in 24 hours
Signs of dehydration — sunken eyes, no urine for 6+ hours, very dry mouth
Infant or elderly person with any diarrhea — higher dehydration risk

name: First Aid for Vomiting
when_to_apply: Single or repeated vomiting episodes; nausea; unable to keep fluids down
immediate_steps: Let the person vomit — do not try to suppress it forcefully
Sit upright or lean forward during vomiting — prevent aspiration
After vomiting — rinse mouth with water; do not eat or drink for 20–30 minutes
Start small sips of water or ORS every 5 minutes — do not give large amounts at once
Gradually progress to clear fluids — coconut water, diluted juice, thin dal
Ondansetron ODT tablet (4 mg) — dissolves on tongue; very effective for moderate vomiting
home_remedies: Adrak (Ginger) tea or ginger chews — most evidence-based natural antiemetic; sip slowly
Jeera (Cumin) and ajwain (Carom) boiled water — traditional Indian remedy; relieves nausea and gut spasm
Nimbu pani (Lemon water) with sendha namak — soothing, replenishes electrolytes; widely used across India
Pudina (Mint) tea — fresh mint leaves boiled in water; natural antiemetic and digestive
Saunf (Fennel) seeds chewed raw — traditional Indian after-meal digestive that also reduces nausea
Cold compress on forehead and back of neck — reduces nausea and provides comfort
do_not_do: Do not give large amounts of fluid immediately — worsens vomiting
Do not give solid food until vomiting stops for 2 hours
Do not give promethazine to children under 2 years
Do not lie flat after vomiting — aspiration risk
go_to_doctor_if: Vomiting blood or coffee-ground material
Vomiting with severe headache or stiff neck — meningitis
Vomiting for more than 24 hours in adults or 12 hours in children
Unable to keep even small sips of water down
Vomiting after head injury

name: First Aid for Nose Bleed (Epistaxis)
when_to_apply: Spontaneous or trauma-related bleeding from nose
immediate_steps: Sit upright and lean slightly forward — prevents blood from going down the throat
Pinch the soft part of nose (below the bony bridge) firmly for 10–15 minutes without releasing
Breathe through mouth
Apply cold compress or ice pack wrapped in cloth to bridge of nose and back of neck
After bleeding stops — do not blow nose for several hours
Avoid hot food, drinks, or exertion for the next few hours
home_remedies: Cold water splash on back of neck — reflex vasoconstriction; traditional Indian first response
Tulsi (Basil) leaves — squeeze 2–3 drops of juice into nostril; traditional hemostatic remedy in Indian homes
Ice pack or cold wet cloth on nose bridge — vasoconstriction reduces bleeding
Nasal saline spray — keeps mucosa moist; prevents recurrent nosebleeds especially in dry weather
do_not_do: Do NOT tilt head backward — blood flows into throat causing nausea, vomiting, or choking
Do not pack nose tightly with cotton wool — sticks to clot and restarts bleeding on removal
Do not release pinch before 10 minutes — breaks clot formation
Do not blow nose for several hours after bleeding stops
go_to_doctor_if: Bleeding does not stop after 20–30 minutes of firm pressure
Recurring nosebleeds — may need cauterization
Nosebleed after head trauma
Nosebleed in person on blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin)
Large blood loss with dizziness or fainting

name: First Aid for Tooth Pain / Dental Emergency
when_to_apply: Sudden severe toothache, knocked-out tooth, broken tooth, dental abscess swelling
immediate_steps: Rinse mouth with warm salt water — reduces inflammation and kills bacteria
For knocked-out permanent tooth — hold by crown (not root); rinse gently in milk; try to reinsert into socket; if not possible — store in milk or saliva; go to dentist within 60 minutes
For severe pain — take ibuprofen 400 mg or paracetamol 500 mg
Apply clove oil (Eugenol) to painful tooth area with cotton ball — natural dental anesthetic
For dental abscess (swelling) — warm salt water rinse every 2 hours; do not lance at home
home_remedies: Laung (Clove) oil — most evidence-based natural dental pain remedy; eugenol in cloves is a natural analgesic and antiseptic; apply directly to tooth
Whole clove — place clove against aching tooth and bite gently; releases eugenol oil
Warm salt water gargle — antibacterial and anti-inflammatory; reduces swelling around infected tooth
Garlic paste — crushed garlic on aching tooth; allicin in garlic has antibacterial properties
Hing (Asafoetida) and lemon juice — 1 pinch hing in lemon juice; applied to tooth; traditional Indian toothache remedy
Onion — raw onion piece chewed or held against aching tooth; antimicrobial
do_not_do: Do not put aspirin directly on the tooth or gum — causes chemical burn to gum tissue
Do not ignore dental abscess — can spread to throat and become life-threatening
Do not reimplant a knocked-out baby (milk) tooth — can damage permanent tooth underneath
Do not use very hot or cold food on sensitive tooth — worsens pain
go_to_doctor_if: Swelling of face or jaw — possible abscess spreading
Fever with toothache — sign of infection
Knocked-out permanent tooth — see dentist within 60 minutes for best chance of saving the tooth
Broken tooth with sharp edge cutting tongue or cheek
Pain not relieved by OTC painkillers

name: First Aid for Sprain and Muscle Strain
when_to_apply: After twisting ankle, wrist, or knee; sudden sharp pain; swelling; inability to bear weight; muscle pulled during exercise
immediate_steps: RICE protocol — Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation
REST — stop activity immediately; do not walk on injured ankle
ICE — apply ice pack (wrapped in cloth) for 15–20 minutes every 2 hours for first 48 hours
COMPRESSION — wrap with crepe bandage (not too tight — check fingers/toes remain pink)
ELEVATION — raise injured limb above heart level to reduce swelling
Take ibuprofen 400 mg or diclofenac 50 mg for pain and swelling
home_remedies: Haldi (Turmeric) and mustard oil paste — warm paste applied to sprained area; reduces swelling; classic Indian home remedy
Castor oil warm compress — warm castor oil applied with cloth wrapped around sprain; anti-inflammatory
Arnica gel (available in Indian pharmacies) — topical anti-bruising and anti-swelling remedy; homeopathic but widely used
Vinegar warm compress — diluted vinegar-soaked cloth on sprain; reduces inflammation
Mahanarayan oil massage — Ayurvedic oil massage around (not on) injured area after 48 hours; promotes healing
do_not_do: Do not apply heat in first 48 hours — increases swelling; switch to heat after 48–72 hours
Do not walk or bear weight on severely sprained ankle — may be fractured
Do not massage directly on acutely sprained joint
Do not wrap compression bandage too tightly — impairs circulation
go_to_doctor_if: Inability to bear any weight after 24 hours — possible fracture
Severe swelling with bruising immediately
Joint deformity or feeling of something tearing at time of injury
Numbness or tingling below injury — nerve involvement
No improvement after 5–7 days of RICE

name: First Aid for Fainting (Syncope)
when_to_apply: Person suddenly loses consciousness briefly; pale, sweaty before fainting; collapse after prolonged standing, heat, or emotional shock
immediate_steps: Lay person flat on their back immediately — do not keep them upright in a chair
Elevate legs above heart level (30–45 degrees) to restore blood flow to brain
Loosen tight clothing — collar, tie, bra strap
Ensure fresh air — open windows, fan, move to cool area
Check breathing and pulse — if absent, start CPR
Person should regain consciousness within 1–2 minutes
Once awake — help them sit up slowly; give water slowly
home_remedies: Smelling salts (Kapoor — camphor) — held under nose; traditional Indian remedy to revive fainting person; ammonia causes reflex breathing
Nimbu pani (Lemon water with salt and sugar) — given after recovery of consciousness; restores electrolytes
Hing (Asafoetida) — strong smell held near nose; traditional Indian smelling salt equivalent
Cold water splash on face — gentle stimulus to revive mildly fainted person
do_not_do: Do not try to prop person upright during fainting — worsens brain blood flow
Do not give anything by mouth while unconscious — choking risk
Do not slap or shake the person aggressively
Do not leave person alone after first faint — another episode may follow
go_to_doctor_if: First fainting episode in anyone over 40
Fainting with chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath
Fainting after exercise
Takes more than 2–3 minutes to recover consciousness
Fainting in a diabetic patient — may be hypoglycemia
Fainting with tongue bite or limb jerking — possible seizure

name: First Aid for Insect Sting / Bee Sting
when_to_apply: Pain, redness, swelling at sting site after bee, wasp, or hornet sting
immediate_steps: Remove bee stinger by scraping sideways with credit card or fingernail — do not pinch with tweezers (squeezes more venom)
Wash the sting site with soap and water
Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth for 10–15 minutes to reduce swelling
Take antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg or chlorpheniramine 4 mg) for itching and swelling
Take ibuprofen or paracetamol for pain
Watch for signs of anaphylaxis for 30 minutes — throat tightness, breathing difficulty, hives all over body
home_remedies: Baking soda paste — 1 tsp baking soda + water applied to sting; neutralizes bee venom (acidic); reduces pain
Onion slice — raw onion applied directly to sting; natural antihistamine enzymes; Indian home remedy
Haldi (Turmeric) paste with coconut oil — applied on sting; reduces swelling and pain
Neem oil — applied to sting area; antibacterial and anti-inflammatory
Mud or clay pack — traditional rural Indian remedy; cooling and draws out venom (clean mud only)
do_not_do: Do not squeeze or pinch stinger out — pushes more venom
Do not scratch the sting — worsens local reaction
Do not ignore throat tightness or breathing difficulty — call 108
Do not apply vinegar to bee sting (vinegar is for jellyfish — wrong for bees)
go_to_doctor_if: Any throat swelling, difficulty breathing, or widespread hives — emergency anaphylaxis
Multiple bee stings (more than 10) — toxic venom load
Stung in throat or mouth
Child with large local reaction
Known bee allergy — always go to hospital regardless of symptoms

name: First Aid for Acid Reflux / Acidity Attack
when_to_apply: Burning sensation in chest after meals; acid taste in mouth; belching; bloating; sour throat
immediate_steps: Sit upright or stand — lying down worsens acid reflux
Take antacid (Digene, Gelusil, or Eno) immediately for relief
Drink cold milk or coconut water — neutralizes stomach acid temporarily
Avoid eating or drinking anything acidic (coffee, tea, citrus) for several hours
If severe — take omeprazole 20 mg or pantoprazole 40 mg if available
home_remedies: Saunf (Fennel) seeds — chew 1 tsp after meals; reduces acid secretion; most common Indian post-meal digestive
Jeera (Cumin) water — boil 1 tsp cumin in water; drink warm; reduces acidity and bloating
Aam panna — raw mango drink with mint; cooling and stomach soothing
Ajwain (Carom seeds) water — boil ajwain in water; drink warm; highly effective for gas and acidity
Baking soda water — 1/2 tsp in 200 mL water; instant alkalizing effect; use occasionally only
Cold milk — glass of plain cold milk; alkaline and neutralizes acid quickly; the classic Indian acidity remedy
Nimbu pani without sugar (with just salt) — though acidic, triggers bicarbonate secretion; paradoxically helps mild acidity
do_not_do: Do not lie down immediately after eating
Do not take antacids with other medicines — absorb them
Do not use baking soda daily — excess sodium and CO2 buildup
Do not ignore chest pain from acidity without ruling out heart attack — especially in men over 40
go_to_doctor_if: Chest pain with sweating or arm pain — rule out heart attack
Acid reflux more than twice a week — GERD requiring PPI therapy
Difficulty swallowing with acidity
Weight loss with acidity — red flag for esophageal disease
Blood in vomit or black stools

name: First Aid for Headache
when_to_apply: Dull, throbbing, or pressure headache; tension headache; mild migraine
immediate_steps: Rest in a quiet, dark, cool room
Drink a glass of water — dehydration is the most common cause of headache
Take paracetamol 500–1000 mg or ibuprofen 400 mg
Apply cold compress on forehead for migraine or hot compress on back of neck for tension headache
Massage temples, neck, and base of skull gently
home_remedies: Amrutanjan / Tiger Balm — classic Indian topical balm; apply to temples and forehead; menthol provides cooling relief
Iodex / Moov balm — applied to forehead and back of neck; commonly used in Indian homes for tension headache
Ginger tea — boil ginger in water with honey; anti-inflammatory; effective for migraines per studies
Peppermint oil — diluted and applied to temples; relieves tension headache as effectively as paracetamol per research
Tulsi and ginger decoction — boil both in water, strain, add honey; traditional Indian headache remedy
Besan (Gram flour) paste with water on forehead — cooling; used in rural India for fever headache
Clove and salt compress — warm cloth with clove oil; applied to temples; traditional remedy
do_not_do: Do not take more than recommended dose of paracetamol or ibuprofen
Do not ignore sudden worst headache of life — thunderclap headache is a medical emergency (subarachnoid hemorrhage)
Do not overuse painkillers — causes medication overuse headache
Do not stare at bright screens during migraine — worsens photophobia
go_to_doctor_if: Sudden severe worst headache you've ever had — emergency
Headache with fever and stiff neck — possible meningitis
Headache with vision changes, confusion, or weakness
Headache after head injury
Headache waking you from sleep or worse in morning

name: First Aid for Electric Shock
when_to_apply: Person in contact with electrical source; burns at contact points; loss of consciousness; muscle spasm from shock
immediate_steps: DO NOT touch the person until power is switched off
Turn off the main power switch or disconnect the source immediately
If cannot turn off power — use non-conducting object (dry wooden stick, plastic chair) to push person away from source
Call 108 immediately — even if person appears fine
Check for breathing and pulse — start CPR if absent
Look for entry and exit burn wounds — treat as burn with cool water
Do not move person with suspected spinal injury from severe shock
home_remedies: No home remedy — electrical injury requires emergency medical evaluation
Cool water on burn entry and exit points — same as burn management after safety is ensured
do_not_do: Do NOT touch the person while they are still in contact with electricity — you will also be electrocuted
Do not use wet objects to disconnect source
Do not assume person is fine because they are conscious — internal injuries and arrhythmia can develop hours later
Do not leave person unmonitored — cardiac arrhythmia can appear late
go_to_doctor_if: All electric shock victims — always, even if apparently fine
Burns at entry and exit points — require wound care
Palpitations, chest pain, or shortness of breath after shock
Confusion or memory loss after shock
Unconsciousness even briefly

name: First Aid for Constipation
when_to_apply: No bowel movement for 2–3 days; hard dry stools; straining; bloating; sense of incomplete evacuation
immediate_steps: Drink 2–3 glasses of warm water immediately on waking — stimulates bowel movement
Walk or do light physical activity — movement stimulates intestinal peristalsis
Take psyllium husk (Isabgol) — 2 tsp in large glass of warm water
Take lactulose syrup 15–30 mL if available
Do not ignore urge to pass stool — postponing worsens constipation
Sit in correct posture on toilet (feet on a small stool — squatting angle improves rectal alignment)
home_remedies: Triphala churna — 1 tsp in warm water at bedtime; the gold standard Ayurvedic bowel regulator; used in Indian homes for generations
Castor oil — 1–2 teaspoons at bedtime; strong natural laxative; used for acute constipation in Indian households
Warm water with lemon and honey — first thing in morning on empty stomach; natural bowel stimulant
Soaked raisins (kismis) — soak overnight, eat with water in morning; natural mild laxative
Anjeer (Fig) — 2–3 soaked figs in water overnight; eat morning; rich in fiber and natural laxative compounds
Papaya — fresh ripe papaya; enzyme papain and high fiber stimulate bowel movement
Ghee in warm milk — 1 tsp ghee in warm milk at bedtime; lubricates colon; classic Indian constipation remedy
do_not_do: Do not use stimulant laxatives (bisacodyl) daily — causes dependence
Do not strain excessively — causes hemorrhoids and anal fissure
Do not ignore constipation lasting more than 2 weeks
Do not give enemas to children at home without guidance
go_to_doctor_if: Constipation with blood in stool
Constipation alternating with diarrhea — possible IBS or colon cancer
Constipation with weight loss or rectal pain
New onset constipation in person over 50
No bowel movement for more than 5 days

name: First Aid for Joint Pain / Arthritis Flare
when_to_apply: Sudden joint pain, stiffness, or swelling; morning joint stiffness; post-exercise joint pain
immediate_steps: Rest the affected joint — avoid activities that worsen pain
Apply ice pack for acute swollen joint (first 48 hours) — 15–20 minutes every 2 hours
Apply heat (warm cloth or heating pad) for stiff joints or chronic pain
Take ibuprofen 400 mg or diclofenac 50 mg with food
Elevate swollen joint if possible
Gentle range-of-motion movement — do not completely immobilize
home_remedies: Haldi (Turmeric) milk with black pepper — 1/2 tsp turmeric + pinch of black pepper in warm milk; curcumin is an evidence-based anti-inflammatory
Ajwain (Carom) seed water — boil 1 tsp ajwain; drink twice daily; traditional Indian joint pain remedy
Mahanarayan oil massage — warm Ayurvedic oil; gentle massage around (not on) inflamed joint; improves circulation
Castor oil pack — warm castor oil applied on cloth wrapped around joint; reduces inflammation
Ginger tea — anti-inflammatory; effective for OA knee pain per clinical studies
Fenugreek (Methi) seeds — soak overnight, eat paste in morning; traditional anti-inflammatory remedy
Mustard oil with camphor massage — warm mustard oil with dissolved camphor applied to joint; widely used in North Indian homes
do_not_do: Do not apply heat to acutely swollen hot joint — worsens inflammation
Do not massage a severely inflamed red hot joint
Do not ignore joint swelling with fever — possible septic arthritis (emergency)
Do not self-inject steroids — joint infections from improper technique are catastrophic
go_to_doctor_if: Hot, red, swollen joint with fever — septic arthritis emergency
Single joint suddenly very painful and swollen — gout or infection
Joint pain with morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour — rheumatoid arthritis
Joint pain after injury — possible fracture or ligament tear

name: Indian Home Remedies — General Immunity and Wellness
when_to_apply: During season change, before cold weather, after illness recovery, general immune boosting
immediate_steps: Maintain hydration — 8 glasses of water daily; warm water preferred in Indian tradition
Eat home-cooked food with haldi, jeera, dhania, adrak, lahsun — all spices with medicinal properties
Daily Surya namaskar (12 rounds) or 30 minutes walking
Sleep 7–8 hours
Avoid cold water during illness — warm beverages always preferred in Indian homes
home_remedies: Morning Kadha — tulsi + ginger + black pepper + cloves + cinnamon boiled in water with honey; the ultimate Indian immunity drink
Chyawanprash — 1–2 tsp daily with warm milk; traditional Ayurvedic formulation with 40+ herbs; enhances immunity; given to children and elderly
Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) juice — 20–30 mL daily on empty stomach; anti-inflammatory and immunity booster; widely used in Indian homes
Amla (Indian Gooseberry) — richest natural source of Vitamin C; 1 fresh amla or 1 tsp amla powder daily
Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) — 1/2 tsp with warm milk at bedtime; adaptogen, stress reducer, immunity booster; WHO-recognized
Haldi (Turmeric) + Milk (Haldi Doodh) — golden milk daily; anti-inflammatory, anti-microbial, immune boosting
Neem leaves — chew 5 fresh neem leaves every morning on empty stomach; blood purifier; antifungal and antibacterial
Tulsi leaves — chew 5 fresh tulsi leaves daily; antimicrobial and adaptogenic
Ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — mix and take 1 tsp daily; antimicrobial; reduces risk of common cold
Pomegranate and beetroot juice — antioxidant-rich; increases hemoglobin and immunity
do_not_do: Do not take Ayurvedic herbs without checking for interactions with prescription medications
Do not replace prescribed medicines with home remedies for serious conditions
Do not give adult herbal preparations to infants without guidance
go_to_doctor_if: Persistent weakness or fatigue despite home remedies
Recurring infections suggesting immune deficiency
Weight loss without trying

name: First Aid for Diabetic Emergency (Hypoglycemia at Home)
when_to_apply: Diabetic patient with sweating, trembling, confusion, palpitations, dizziness, or loss of consciousness
immediate_steps: If conscious — give 15 g fast-acting glucose immediately: 4–5 glucose tablets, 150 mL fruit juice, 1 tsp sugar dissolved in water, or 3 tsp honey
Recheck blood sugar after 15 minutes — if still low, repeat glucose
Once blood sugar normal — give a proper meal or snack with complex carbohydrates
If unconscious — do NOT give anything by mouth; place in recovery position; rub glucagon gel or honey on gums if available; call 108
Give glucagon injection if available and trained
home_remedies: Sugar water / glucose water — fastest home treatment; dissolve 2–3 tsp sugar in 100 mL water; drink immediately
Gud (Jaggery) water — dissolve small piece of jaggery in water; traditional Indian quick glucose source
Banana — 1–2 bananas; fast natural sugar source; widely available in Indian homes
Coconut water with sugar — natural electrolytes plus glucose; used in Indian homes for sugar recovery
do_not_do: Do not give anything by mouth to unconscious person
Do not give protein or fat foods for immediate low sugar — they work too slowly
Do not skip a meal after hypoglycemia — follow up with full meal
Do not delay treatment — brain damage occurs within minutes of severe hypoglycemia
go_to_doctor_if: Unconscious from hypoglycemia — emergency
Hypoglycemia not responding after two glucose doses
Severe or recurrent hypoglycemia — medication dose adjustment needed
First hypoglycemic episode — doctor needs to review insulin or medication dose

name: First Aid for Stroke (Brain Attack)
when_to_apply: Use FAST test: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 108
immediate_steps: Use FAST — Face drooping + Arm weakness + Speech slurred = call 108 immediately
Note time when symptoms started — crucial for clot-busting treatment (tPA given within 4.5 hours)
Do NOT give aspirin (unlike heart attack — stroke may be hemorrhagic and aspirin worsens it)
Lay person on their side if unconscious — recovery position
Keep airway clear — remove dentures, do not give anything to eat or drink
Stay calm and reassure person — anxiety raises blood pressure and worsens stroke
home_remedies: No home remedy for acute stroke — every minute of delay = millions of brain cells lost
Prevention: Arjuna bark tea, garlic daily, turmeric milk — traditional Indian cardiovascular protective practices
do_not_do: Do not give aspirin — stroke may be hemorrhagic (bleeding), aspirin would be fatal
Do not give food or water — swallowing reflex may be impaired
Do not leave person alone
Do not wait to see if symptoms pass — act on first sign; even if symptoms resolve (TIA), emergency evaluation is needed
go_to_doctor_if: Any FAST symptom — immediately without delay
Sudden severe headache (worst of life) — possible hemorrhagic stroke
Sudden vision loss in one eye
Sudden loss of balance or coordination

name: First Aid for Food Poisoning
when_to_apply: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps within hours of eating; fever; weakness
immediate_steps: Rest and let vomiting clear the toxin — do not suppress initial vomiting
Start ORS (oral rehydration solution) once vomiting reduces — small sips every 5 minutes
Take ondansetron ODT 4 mg if available to control severe vomiting
Take loperamide (Imodium) 4 mg for diarrhea once vomiting is controlled — only if no blood in stool
Eat light foods once tolerated — khichdi, curd rice, banana, toast
Avoid solid food for 6–8 hours; liquid diet only initially
home_remedies: Ginger and honey tea — boil ginger, add honey; antiemetic and antimicrobial; soothes gut
Jeera (Cumin) and ajwain (Carom) boiled water — powerful gut antiseptic and antispasmodic; classic Indian food poisoning remedy
Tulsi juice — 5–10 ml fresh tulsi juice; antimicrobial against common food pathogens
Rice kanji (rice water with salt) — restores fluids and electrolytes; gentle on inflamed gut
Curd (yogurt) with rice — probiotics help restore gut flora after food poisoning
Pomegranate rind tea — boil dried pomegranate skin in water; astringent; traditional Indian remedy for food poisoning diarrhea
do_not_do: Do not take antibiotics without medical advice — most food poisoning is viral or self-limiting
Do not suppress early vomiting — it is clearing the toxin
Do not eat dairy, fatty, or spicy foods until fully recovered
Do not use loperamide if blood or mucus in stool
go_to_doctor_if: Blood in stool or vomit
Symptoms in very young child, elderly, or immunocompromised person
Fever above 38.5°C
Unable to keep any fluid down for 12–24 hours
Suspected mushroom, seafood, or botulism poisoning — always emergency

name: First Aid for Minor Cuts
when_to_apply: Small superficial cuts from knife, glass, paper, or sharp object; minor bleeding that slows with pressure; no deep tissue visible
immediate_steps: Wash hands with soap and water before touching the cut
Rinse cut under clean running water for 1–2 minutes to remove dirt and debris
Apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth or gauze for 3–5 minutes to stop bleeding
Once bleeding stops — apply antiseptic (Dettol diluted 1:20 with water, or Savlon, or diluted povidone-iodine)
Cover with a sterile adhesive bandage (Band-Aid) or small gauze + tape
Change dressing daily or when wet or dirty
home_remedies: Haldi (Turmeric) powder — sprinkle a pinch on the cut after rinsing; natural antimicrobial and clotting agent; the most classic Indian mom first aid
Honey (raw) — apply a thin layer on clean cut; antimicrobial and promotes healing; used in Indian homes before antiseptic was common
Aloe vera gel — fresh gel from leaf applied after cleaning; cools, soothes, and prevents infection
Neem leaf paste — crushed fresh neem leaves pressed on cut; antibacterial; used in South Indian homes
Coconut oil — a drop on clean minor cut; antimicrobial and skin-healing lauric acid content
do_not_do: Do not use cotton wool directly on cut — fibers stick to wound and restart bleeding on removal
Do not blow on the cut — mouth bacteria cause infection
Do not apply undiluted Dettol or alcohol directly — kills healing cells and causes unnecessary pain
Do not remove the scab when it forms — natural wound seal
go_to_doctor_if: Cut is deep, gaping, or longer than 1–2 cm — may need stitches
Bleeding does not stop after 10–15 minutes of firm pressure
Cut from rusty object or animal scratch — tetanus risk
Signs of infection appear — increasing redness, swelling, warmth, pus after 24–48 hours
Cut on face, hand, or near a joint — specialist care recommended

name: First Aid for Minor Burns
when_to_apply: Small superficial burn from hot utensil, hot liquid splash, brief flame contact, or steam; redness, mild pain, no large blisters, area smaller than palm
immediate_steps: Cool the burn under cool (not ice cold) running water for 10–20 minutes — the single most important step
Remove rings, bangles, or tight clothing near the burned area before swelling begins
After cooling — cover loosely with clean cling film or a clean non-fluffy cloth
Take paracetamol 500 mg or ibuprofen 400 mg for pain
Do not burst small blisters — they protect against infection
Apply a soothing burn gel (Burnol, aloe vera) after the cooling phase
home_remedies: Aloe vera gel — fresh gel from leaf applied after cooling the burn; cooling, anti-inflammatory, and promotes skin healing; widely used across Indian homes
Burnol (Turmeric-based burn ointment) — Indian pharmacy staple; apply after cooling phase; antimicrobial
Raw potato slice — placed on minor burn after cooling; starch and moisture provides relief; traditional Indian remedy
Coconut oil — applied after 24 hours on healed minor burns; moisturizes and reduces scarring
Cold milk compress — cotton soaked in cold milk; mildly cooling and soothing for very minor burns in Indian homes
do_not_do: Do not apply toothpaste, butter, ghee, oil, or ice — traps heat, causes infection, worsens damage
Do not use ice or ice-cold water — causes frostbite and additional tissue damage
Do not burst blisters — removes natural protective barrier
Do not apply cotton wool directly — fibers stick to burn surface
go_to_doctor_if: Burn larger than the person's palm size
Burn on face, hands, feet, genitals, or over joints — always specialist care
Deep burns — white, brown, or black (painless) skin indicates serious depth
Burns in children, diabetics, or elderly — always evaluate
Blisters larger than 2 cm or infected-looking burn

name: First Aid for Indigestion
when_to_apply: After eating — feeling of fullness, bloating, upper abdominal discomfort, nausea, belching, heaviness in stomach, gas
immediate_steps: Sit upright — do not lie down after eating; this is the most common mistake worsening indigestion
Walk slowly for 10–15 minutes after a heavy meal — promotes gastric emptying
Take an antacid (Digene, Gelusil, or Eno) if available
Sip warm water slowly — helps break down food and ease gut movement
Loosen tight clothing around abdomen — belt or waistband compression worsens bloating
Gentle clockwise abdominal massage — follows direction of colon; relieves gas and bloating
home_remedies: Saunf (Fennel seeds) — chew 1 tsp after meals; most common Indian post-meal digestive; relieves bloating and gas effectively
Ajwain (Carom seeds) with black salt — 1/2 tsp ajwain + pinch kala namak in warm water; the number one Indian remedy for indigestion; relieves gas in minutes
Jeera (Cumin) water — boil 1 tsp cumin in water, strain, drink warm; stimulates digestive enzymes
Hing (Asafoetida) water — small pinch of hing dissolved in warm water; powerful antiflatulent; used in every Indian kitchen for stomach discomfort
Pudina (Mint) chutney or tea — fresh mint relieves indigestion and nausea; commonly served in Indian homes after rich meals
Ginger juice with honey — 1 tsp ginger juice + 1 tsp honey; stimulates digestive juices
Paan (Betel leaf) without tobacco — traditional post-meal Indian digestive; aids digestion and freshens breath
do_not_do: Do not eat more to feel better — overeating worsens indigestion
Do not lie down for at least 2 hours after eating
Do not take antacids with other medications — interferes with absorption
Do not drink carbonated drinks — temporarily feels good but worsens bloating
go_to_doctor_if: Indigestion lasting more than 2 weeks despite home treatment
Pain that radiates to back or chest
Indigestion with unexplained weight loss — red flag
Vomiting blood or black tarry stools
New indigestion in person over 50 — rule out stomach cancer

name: First Aid for Hyperacidity
when_to_apply: Sour burping, burning sensation in stomach and chest, acid taste in mouth, nausea from excess acid, stomach pain after spicy food or empty stomach
immediate_steps: Drink a glass of plain cold milk — alkaline protein buffers excess stomach acid quickly; fastest home relief
Take an antacid immediately — Digene gel, Gelusil, or Eno sachet in water
Sit upright — do not lie down
Avoid all trigger foods — spicy food, tea, coffee, citrus, tomato
If antacid not available — take omeprazole 20 mg or pantoprazole 40 mg if on hand
Eat a small light meal — empty stomach worsens hyperacidity
home_remedies: Cold milk — a large glass of plain cold milk; most popular Indian home remedy for hyperacidity; immediate buffering effect
Banana — ripe banana coats stomach lining and neutralizes acid; alkaline fruit
Coconut water — naturally alkaline; cools and soothes acidic stomach; very popular in South and West India
Saunf (Fennel) and mishri water — boil fennel seeds with rock sugar, cool, drink; traditional Indian acid remedy
Aam panna (Raw mango cooler) — prevents hyperacidity in summer; balances stomach acids
Gulkand (Rose petal jam) — 1 tsp gulkand with cold milk; Ayurvedic cooling remedy for acidity and pitta
Amla (Indian gooseberry) juice — 20 mL on empty stomach; regulates acid secretion; rich in Vitamin C
do_not_do: Do not take antacids with meals — take 1–2 hours after
Do not consume tea, coffee, or alcohol on empty stomach
Do not use baking soda daily — sodium overload and rebound acidity
Do not confuse hyperacidity chest burn with heart attack pain — if in doubt, seek emergency care
go_to_doctor_if: Hyperacidity more than 2–3 times a week — GERD requiring PPI treatment
Burning sensation reaching throat or mouth regularly
Night-time acid waking you up — erosive esophagitis risk
Chest pain with acid — rule out cardiac cause in high-risk individuals
Difficulty swallowing with hyperacidity

name: First Aid for Piles (Hemorrhoids — Minor)
when_to_apply: Mild rectal bleeding (bright red on toilet paper), itching around anus, mild discomfort while sitting or passing stool, external soft lump near anus
immediate_steps: Sitz bath — sit in warm shallow water (not hot) for 10–15 minutes 2–3 times a day; reduces swelling and discomfort
Apply over-the-counter hemorrhoid cream (Proctosedyl, Faktu, or Anovate) to affected area
Use moist toilet paper or water jet (Indian-style bidet/health faucet) — never dry rough paper
Increase fiber intake immediately — fruits, vegetables, isabgol
Drink 8–10 glasses of water daily
Avoid straining on toilet — use footstool to adopt squatting position which straightens anorectal angle
home_remedies: Sitz bath with rock salt — warm water with sendha namak; reduces inflammation and itching; classic Indian home treatment
Coconut oil — applied externally to anal area; lubricates and soothes; anti-inflammatory
Aloe vera gel — applied topically around anus; cooling and anti-inflammatory; widely used in Indian homes
Triphala churna — 1 tsp in warm water at bedtime; Ayurvedic bowel regulator that prevents straining; foundational Indian remedy for piles
Radish (Mooli) juice — 50–60 mL fresh radish juice with honey twice daily; traditional Indian pile management remedy
Witch hazel (pads or lotion) — astringent; reduces swelling and bleeding; available at Indian pharmacies
do_not_do: Do not strain while passing stool — most important factor in worsening piles
Do not sit on toilet for more than 5 minutes — reduces rectal vein congestion
Do not use dry or rough toilet paper — causes bleeding and irritation
Do not ignore rectal bleeding — always confirm it is from piles and not something more serious
go_to_doctor_if: Bleeding that is heavy or does not stop after stool
Pain so severe sitting is impossible — possible thrombosed hemorrhoid needing drainage
Prolapsed hemorrhoid that does not reduce back inside
Any rectal bleeding in person over 40 — rule out colorectal cancer
No improvement after 1–2 weeks of home treatment

name: First Aid for Urinary Tract Infection — Early/Mild
when_to_apply: Burning sensation while urinating, increased urge to urinate frequently, cloudy or slightly smelly urine, mild lower abdominal discomfort; no fever or back pain yet
immediate_steps: Drink large amounts of water immediately — 2–3 litres per day to flush bacteria from bladder
Urinate frequently — do not hold urine; each void flushes more bacteria
Take OTC urinary analgesic (Phenazopyridine / Pyridium) if available — relieves burning sensation
Take cranberry juice or extract if available
Avoid coffee, alcohol, and spicy food — irritate the bladder
Seek medical care for antibiotic prescription — UTI does not fully resolve without antibiotics in most cases
home_remedies: Plenty of water — most important; 3–4 litres daily; dilutes bacteria and flushes bladder; basic Indian advice
Barley water (Jau pani) — boil barley grains in water, cool, drink throughout day; traditional Indian diuretic and bladder soother
Coconut water — natural diuretic and electrolyte; widely used in India for urinary complaints
Coriander (Dhania) seed water — soak 1 tbsp seeds overnight, strain, drink morning; traditional Indian UTI remedy with mild antimicrobial properties
Cranberry juice — unsweetened; prevents bacterial adhesion to bladder wall; evidence-based natural UTI preventive
Gulkand in milk — cooling Ayurvedic remedy; reduces burning sensation from UTI in Indian tradition
do_not_do: Do not hold urine — holding provides bacteria time to multiply
Do not take someone else's leftover antibiotics — wrong drug, wrong dose
Do not ignore symptoms hoping they resolve — uncomplicated UTI can ascend to kidney infection
Do not use public toilets without hygiene precautions — common source of UTI
go_to_doctor_if: Fever above 38°C with UTI symptoms — kidney infection (pyelonephritis)
Back or flank pain with burning urination
Symptoms not improving after 24–48 hours of increased fluids
Blood in urine
UTI in men, children, or pregnant women — always requires medical evaluation
Recurrent UTIs (more than 2 in 6 months) — investigation needed

name: First Aid for Sunstroke / Sunstroke Seasonal
when_to_apply: After prolonged sun exposure — headache, dizziness, nausea, profuse sweating, fatigue, weakness; body temperature raised but person is still sweating (heat exhaustion stage before heat stroke)
immediate_steps: Move the person immediately to shade or cool indoor area
Lay them down and elevate feet slightly — improves blood flow to brain
Remove excess clothing and fan the person
Apply cool wet cloths to forehead, neck, and armpits
Give cool water or ORS to drink in small sips if conscious — not ice-cold water
Spray cool water on skin and fan simultaneously — most effective evaporative cooling
Monitor temperature — if above 40°C and person stops sweating or becomes confused, call 108 immediately (heat stroke)
home_remedies: Aam panna (Raw mango cooler) — boil raw mango, pulp with cumin, black pepper, salt, sugar, mint, water; the most effective traditional Indian sun and heat illness drink; prevents and treats mild sunstroke
Buttermilk (Chaas) with jeera and salt — cooling probiotic drink; replenishes electrolytes; the classic Indian summer remedy used widely across all regions
Kokum sherbet — dilute kokum with water and sugar; cooling; popular in Maharashtra and Goa for heat
Onion on back of neck — sliced raw onion applied to back of neck and soles of feet; traditional Indian folk remedy for sunstroke believed to draw out heat
Sattu (roasted gram flour) sharbat — sattu mixed in cold water with salt, lemon, and jeera; Bihar and UP traditional cooling summer drink
Neem water bath — neem leaves boiled in water, cooled; used as cooling bath water after sunstroke in Indian rural areas
do_not_do: Do not give tea, coffee, or alcohol — diuretics that worsen dehydration
Do not give ice-cold water to drink rapidly — causes stomach cramping
Do not give aspirin or paracetamol — sunstroke is not an infection-based fever; these do not help
Do not leave person in hot car or direct sun even for a minute
Do not ignore — heat exhaustion can progress to life-threatening heat stroke within 30 minutes
go_to_doctor_if: Temperature above 40°C (104°F) — heat stroke emergency
Person stops sweating despite being hot — dangerous sign
Confusion, slurred speech, or seizure from heat
Vomiting or inability to drink fluids
Child or elderly person with any heat illness — hospital evaluation required

name: First Aid for Loss of Appetite
when_to_apply: Not feeling hungry, unable to eat normal portions, disinterest in food, skipping meals, post-illness poor appetite, weakness from not eating
immediate_steps: Eat small frequent meals — 5–6 small portions rather than 3 big meals
Start with light easy-to-digest foods — khichdi, daliya, curd rice, fruit
Drink water or coconut water before meals — hydration improves appetite
Avoid eating while watching screens — distraction reduces mindful eating
Light 10-minute walk before meals — physical activity stimulates appetite
Zinc deficiency is a common cause — consider zinc supplement (20 mg/day) if loss persists
home_remedies: Ajwain and black salt water — 1/2 tsp ajwain + pinch kala namak in warm water before meals; stimulates digestive fire (agni); classic Ayurvedic appetite stimulant
Ginger juice with rock salt — few drops on tongue before meal; stimulates saliva and gastric acid; widely used in India
Triphala churna — 1 tsp in warm water at bedtime; regulates digestion and stimulates appetite by next morning
Trikatu churna (dry ginger + black pepper + long pepper) — Ayurvedic formulation; stimulates digestive enzymes; traditional Indian appetite remedy
Lemon pickle (Nimbu achar) or raw lemon juice — sour taste stimulates appetite; used widely in Indian homes
Pomegranate with black pepper and honey — traditional Indian remedy to stimulate appetite in recovering patients
Coriander and cumin seeds water — boil together, strain, drink before meals; traditional Indian digestive stimulant
do_not_do: Do not force-feed — causes aversion and worsens the problem
Do not eat processed or junk food to compensate — further disrupts gut
Do not ignore prolonged loss of appetite (beyond 1–2 weeks) — may indicate underlying illness
Do not skip meals entirely — leads to further metabolic slowdown
go_to_doctor_if: Loss of appetite with unexplained weight loss — red flag for cancer or chronic disease
Persisting more than 2 weeks
In children with growth failure
Accompanied by fever, night sweats, or fatigue
Post-chemotherapy or post-major illness — needs nutritional support plan

name: First Aid for Choking (Mild — Partial Obstruction)
when_to_apply: Person is coughing forcefully, can speak or cry, can breathe (noisy), some air passing; NOT complete airway blockage
immediate_steps: Encourage the person to cough forcefully — the most effective way to dislodge a mild obstruction
Do not interfere with their coughing — it is the body's best mechanism
Stay with the person and reassure them calmly
If coughing is effective and object dislodges — give sips of water
If coughing weakens and person cannot breathe — escalate to full back blows and Heimlich maneuver immediately
For a child — allow natural coughing first; back blows only if cough weakens
home_remedies: Warm water sips — between coughs; helps loosen food particle
A gentle pat on the upper back (not forceful blow) — encourages coughing in mild choking
Honey and warm water — after object clears; soothes irritated throat
Leaning forward while coughing — gravity assists dislodgment; instinctive in Indian home management
do_not_do: Do not do back blows or Heimlich if person is still coughing effectively — may dislodge object deeper
Do not give water if airway is not yet clear — may worsen blockage
Do not leave person alone — obstruction can worsen rapidly
Do not perform blind finger sweep in mouth — may push object deeper
go_to_doctor_if: Coughing weakens or stops — immediate escalation needed
Object felt to be still in throat after clearing
Any breathing difficulty, voice change, or stridor after choking episode
Child who has swallowed a small object — X-ray to confirm passage
Prolonged coughing or pain in throat after choking

name: First Aid for Short Seizure (Post-Seizure Home Care)
when_to_apply: Known epileptic patient has a brief seizure (under 3–5 minutes) that stops on its own; person is now post-ictal (drowsy, confused, tired after seizure)
immediate_steps: After seizure stops — place person in recovery position (on their side) to prevent choking
Clear the area of hard objects — check for injury from fall
Stay with the person and speak calmly — they will be confused (post-ictal state); reassure them
Do not offer food or water until person is fully alert and can swallow safely
Let them rest and sleep — post-ictal fatigue is normal and may last 30–60 minutes
Note seizure duration, type of movements, and any injury — report to neurologist
Check if person took their anti-epileptic medication today — missed dose is the most common trigger
home_remedies: Quiet recovery environment — reduce noise, dim lights; post-ictal brain is hypersensitive
Warm water when alert — hydration after seizure; gentle warm water with sugar if exhausted
Shankhpushpi and Brahmi — traditional Ayurvedic nervine tonics used as long-term maintenance in Indian homes (not during or after acute seizure)
Adequate sleep — most important recovery aid; sleep deprivation is a common seizure trigger
do_not_do: Do not give anything by mouth until fully conscious and alert
Do not restrain the person during or after seizure
Do not leave person alone immediately after seizure — second seizure possible
Do not drive the person yourself if you are shaken — call family or taxi
go_to_doctor_if: Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes — status epilepticus emergency; call 108
Person does not regain normal consciousness within 30 minutes
Injury occurred during the seizure (head injury, fracture)
First-ever seizure in the person
Two or more seizures within 24 hours
